sentences.text: The only thing-- materialistic.
You couldn't do the things that you did when-- before the war because they were controlling everybody-- as their money, as their behavior whatever it is.
Russians like to control people.
So they did.
But then, when the war started between the Germans and Russians, things started to get worse.
And we knew because the Russians were running back, meaning that the Germans were going forward.
The Russian were going back.

As they were running back, the Russians, the young Russians that were in the army, tried to get people to accept them to work for them in our village because otherwise, the Germans would catch them and take them in their lagers.
So there were a lot of nice young people, Russians, that settled down in our village.
It's very important because I was going to point out that I had a very close friend that I loved very dearly, and he was one of them.
This is a Russian young man.
And his name was [?
Kola. ?] So I thought I will point that out because he helped us a lot.
Not all of them.
Just a few of them.
Just a few.
And the rest had to go run back to Russia.
They all did.
The whole army backed up.
And they went back to Russia.
It was summer.
I remember very well.
Right.
Right, right, right, right.
We knew everything about the Germans, and we were acquainted with the politics.
Not-- when I say, 'we'-- my father.
And we knew that we expecting hardship, but never expected something as horrible as this.
No.
We were at--
Yeah.
No, the usual-- the mother and father in Stadniki.
We were studying in-- in Ostrog.
So we continued with the same system.
Up till '41.
In '41, we closed up the house in Ostrog.
Actually, not closed up but left it there.
And we lived in Stadniki.
There is no education.
We started to look for different jobs.
But that came after the Germans took over our village.
They came on a-- motorcycles.
And if I tell you that there were two motorcycles, that's all.
They took over the village.
Two motorcycles.
One was in one part of Stadniki, the other one in the other side, part.
And they called everybody in for a meeting.
We are here to stay.
We'll send you a gentleman in charge.
And they did.
They sent one German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to settle down.
He came with a girlfriend or wife.
And they settled down in Stadniki.
Right.
That would be Ukrainians.
Ukrainians done all the jobs for them that they wanted because, obviously, not Jews.
Ukrainians.
And then on, it started-- the hardships started.
I and my older sister started to go earn some money.
We went to work for-- every day in the morning and come back home and bring whatever we made.
Everything that you could think of-- to help a person in the house, to watch the children, to do in field work-- I was doing.
My sister couldn't, but I did.
So whatever they wanted.
As a  matter of fact, some of them even called in advance, that come tomorrow.
I need you for that.
And the others used to wait for our call.
Ukrainian families in Stadnik.
Very well.
As a matter of fact, they were generous.
They send home goods to eat and other little things.
They're generous.
I had no complaints about that.
But we worked very hard.
Never in our lives did we work so hard.
And mother and father could not work because my mother and father were a little bit older.
And the little ones couldn't work.
So actually, my brother also helped out.
My little sister was taken, to-- not far, to a Ukrainian family.
And they treated her like a daughter.
My brother was taken to another family and was treated like a son.
What they did is the usual, what a child would do.
Take care of-- I don't know-- pigs, to take the cows out-- other things that were connected.
And these houses, mainly, these houses were not in villages.
They were scattered that, in case you see somebody coming, there's plenty of time to hide.
Of the children, yeah.
That was in '41.
And we were immediately, before the first [NON-ENGLISH], which was in Ostrog in 1941, I think, August.
So a month before, like July, we were all gathered and sent to Ostrog.
He knew about it, that they'll be an [NON-ENGLISH].
No, no, no.
The-- the German got orders to send us out.
And all the Jews from villages came to Ostrog.
And we settled down in our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We couldn't do too much.
We didn't have any means of living.

So my mother went to the highest person, German person, in Ostrog, asked him, can she please go back with her family to Stadniki so she can earn a legitimate living and help out the mill not to be ruined?
He agreed to it and sent us back towards-- to Stadniki.
And then we were there for about maybe two months.
Then again-- they-- no-- there was-- no, I'm sorry.
We were there.
And the first [NON-ENGLISH], my sister was killed with her family.
The oldest sister, Mancia, with her family.
We were in-- in Stadniki.
Then we went.
Then there was a second [NON-ENGLISH].
And yet, we still didn't go to Ostrog.
The Germans, somehow, was-- I have to tell you, he was a nice person.
He-- he did not send us for these [NON-ENGLISH].
And he probably needed my father in the mill, too.
So here, it worked both ways.
And we knew that there was another [NON-ENGLISH].
We expected horrible things happen.
And it was leading towards the third one.
The third [NON-ENGLISH] was in '42.
And-- in '42.
And at that time, we were sent to Ostrog.
And that time, we did.
As we came like a few days after, we heard that there is a commotion in the streets, that the Germans are gathering all the left over Jews, and they were marching through the street that we lived on.
As we ran to the window-- I don't remember who-- we spotted that the Jews were marching, and the Germans were guiding them.
Each German was running into the house, getting all the Jews out, and continued.
Now, we were like in the background, between two houses.
And it was on a little hill.
We had an L-shaped storage room.
We opened all the doors and the windows, and we hid in that storage room.
Now, they did come in, the Germans, into the house.
They looked around.
They saw the windows and everything is open.
They looked in that storage room, but did not look into the L-shape.
And he left us.
Then-- when we started to go underground.
This is it.
No more staying too long in the village.
During the night, we slept out in the fields when it was nice.
And when it was cold, we were at our neighbor's in Stadniki.
And then came a day that they-- they decided that the few Jews that slept in Stadniki must be killed.
Our people would not dare to do that.
They were helping us.
They were guiding us.
And they would never do such a thing.
So from outside, three kilometer or four kilometer, there was a village.
And from that village came polizia, or you call it militia-- Ukrainians that we didn't know, and asked where live-- the Jews lived.
We were all in the house eating breakfast.
In few minutes, a Ukrainian, a friend of ours, came and screamed, there are police that's looking for you.
Run for cover.
We all did.
The-- only the little ones and the old ones could not run away.
So my aunt, which was very old-- my father was deaf.
He didn't hear that.
And the little niece that I had, that was killed, also, then-- she was a little baby.
She was-- she was not there.
She was playing outside.
And my younger brother, with a cousin, ran.
And they were shooting after them.
So they couldn't catch them.
So three of them-- one they killed, a cousin of mine.
But these two were very good swimmers, and they ran to the river, fell into the river, and they thought that they drowned.
They left them alone.
They came back, took the little baby.
She was, I think, two and a half, three years old.
And my father and the aunt-- and they took them to the outskirts of Stadniki.
Over there, there was a deep-- not a dumpster, but that deep hole that people used to take some yellow ground, for some reason, to paint the houses, whatever.
They killed them.
And they left them in that ditch.
All the Ukrainian were afraid to bury them.
But that friend of mine, like I told you, that [?
Kola, ?] risk his life, and he gave them a burial there.
After that, our life was completely broken.
My mother was hiding on a roof in one house.
My brother with his family, to the neighbor home-- neighborhood village.
My brother was on the roof-- on the attic, and his wife was working in the fields.
My sisters-- one went to Shepetovka.
The other one went, again, to these people in the scattered houses.
And they were taking care of cows, whatever, and the family.
So we were completely out of Stadniki.
I went to a neighborhood-- it's not a village, again.
It's like scattered houses.
I took care of a little child and was there till they found out that I was there.
Then it came my turn.
They decided it's time for me to be killed.
Then again, there was-- they called him Plenick.
The-- the-- Russian soldier that decided to leave, he lived there, also, with me, with these people.
He found out that there is orders that I should be killed in the last minute.
I had only time to take a little cover, whatever I wore, and I started to run at night.
I ran to my father and my mother, which were hiding not far away.
But as I was walking towards my father and mother-- and there were woods, and I heard talking, which is unusual at night.
I figured that these are the people that came to look for me and kill me.
They did pass by me very close.
Since it was fall, I managed to lie down very flat and cover myself with leaves and lived through till they passed.
When they passed, I continued to go to my mother and joined them there for a very short while.
The person that I told you, that Russian, [INAUDIBLE], they tortured him because they thought that he knows where I am.
And they hanged him because of me.
So that was the end-- that I decide that this is the time that I should part.
When I said, mother and father-- forgive me, it's only mother because father was not alive.
I went, straight-- packed myself, and went straight to a family, Ukrainian, that lived near Kiev, not far away from Kiev.
I knew one of their members.
She lived in Stadniki and worked in Stadniki.
And we were very friendly.
So I walked there.
And I was accepted there.
I was staying there till they had enough food.
After they had no food, there was no place for me to stay.
The only thing I felt-- to go back to other people.
But then, I was worried because my looks were Semitic looks.
I think that the language didn't help too much.
But I heard them talking about partisans, that our partisans are forming, that they accepting members.
They want all the young man to join them.
But when came to female, it was hard.
They couldn't-- they didn't-- they were not willing to accept too many females.
But obviously, since it was my life and death, I tried to meet.
And I did meet one of the partisans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met him.
He right away, fell in love with me, of course.
And when I told him that I'll be his friend, and who knows, after the war, maybe we'll get married, he took me there but expected favors in return.
When I joined the partisans, they were so nice that they actually made him aware that he cannot be my boyfriend because I don't wish so.
And that was it.
The only thing I forgot to tell you is that my friend, [?
Kola, ?] he was already in the partisans.
He went for a very important mission with other partisans.
And he was caught by the Germans.
And not caught red-handed, but he went to sleep over in somebody's house-- actually, not house-- where they store their winter food.
And as were they sleeping there, they were burned alive because one of the Ukrainians told them where they were.
Otherwise they would not.
So he died, and with him, my hope for the partisans because he was going to take me with him to the partisans.
So after that, I had no hope.
But in my mind, I felt the only way for me is to go to partisans.
And that's when I joined the partisans.
What was the name of the group?
Shirlov. ?]
Mainly Ukrainians and Russians.
Russian Ukrainians.
There were no Jews.
No.
And if they knew, if the Jews were there, then you couldn't-- you couldn't know.
Because I, as being Jewish, recognized two members.
I knew, in my heart, that they were Jewish.
Maybe they knew, also.
But we never exchanged information.
They didn't ask you for any papers or proof of--
No.
No.
They couldn't possibly do it because I had-- first of all, I had them, and they were not Jewish.
I was Sophia Shman on the Ukrainian papers.
My mother paid money for-- to-- in the church.
And lead-- church leader gave her dead girl's certificate.
Now, we're talking before '43, when I started to run around loose.
The end of '42.
Or the--
'42, beginning '43.
The first [NON-ENGLISH] was 3,000.
And I'm giving you rough amounts, numbers.
And the second one was two and a half thousand.
The third one was the biggest, also over 3,000.
They killed them.
And they had one grave where he-- they put them to rest.
Shot them, yeah.
They shot them and put them to rest in this big, massive-- in Ostrog.
It was not in Ostrog.
It was suburbia of Ostrog, woods-- in woods.
Right.
Then they needed some lady in little hospitals that we called.
We had a little emergency room.
And there was one lady, but we were very busy with the people that used to come half-dead, practically.
And we had quite a few people that were very sick.
The doctor himself could not manage.
So we had doctor with the nurse.
But then, they liked me, and they took me as a nurse.
So I was working with them as a nurse.
Where did you live?
Woods.
Everybody in the woods when it was warm or half warm.
Three seasons in the woods-- wintertime we had a break, so we occupied a village.
And we took a bath, had a hot meal, and then continue.
Not exactly.
No, we took it by force.
So we were very careful.
But there was such close incidents, that I remember one-- that we had to cross a-- a little road that the Germans were in charge and were there watching.

So the pressure was so hard to get through this point, that when we saw that the Germans far away, we had to move the horses with the wagons as fast as possible without a shrink of noise, without a anything.
That was something I'll never forget.
It was such a hard thing to do.
And I was with the sick ones.
And they were on the wagon, and we were not allowed to leave them.
So I was in the predicament that here we am-- are because any other soldiers or anyone on the feet, you can go back up, you can run, you can do.
We were stuck, three of us, with two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of carriages.
And we had to go through it.
And thank God, somehow we made it.
But it was such a nerve wrack-- nerve wracking because you were right near the soldiers.
You were right there, under their the fire.
So this is how close I came to very big danger there.
And then, when they used to go planting mines or any other things, they used to take one of us to help out in fields.
So when they were meeting Germans and fighting, we were helping.
Did you carry a gun?
Yeah.
I knew how to shoot.
I had a gun.
And--
Oh, gee.
It's a rifle.
We did not have the little ones, so rifle.
A Russian.
All the ammunition was Russian.
So it was a Russian rifle.
So over there, we had-- there were nights and days that I slept in water-- not slept.
I was standing there for hours in the water because you have to accomplish things.
And sometimes, we used to come back with very sick and wounded people.
So that was the-- up to the liberation, I would say, that I was with him.
It was a small one.
Very small.
And thank God that they were forming then, so I could join them.
I would say, maybe a thousand or fifteen hundred.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
Mixed.
Mixed.
As a matter of fact, we had as-- as young as 17, 16.
You know, so they were mixed.
Older were pretty old, too.
I would say, at least 50 years old-- well, I don't know. 50, 60.
OK.
Maybe they looked old, but they were old.
No.
No families.
I would say, half and half.
Or maybe, Russian-- more Russian.
More Russian, I think.
Yeah.
From Russia and other partisans and killing Germans and getting a lot of ammunition.
So these were our sources.
And clothes-- we tried to get from villages.
From villages.
Yeah.
